summary: Bengali-American author known for her short story collection Interpreter of Maladies and novels The Namesake and The Lowland. Her work explores the Indian-American immigrant experience. She later moved to Rome and began writing exclusively in Italian.

works.name: Interpreter of Maladies (title story)
works.description: The title story of Lahiri's debut collection, in which the tour guide Mr. Kapasi takes Mr. and Mrs. Das and their children on a tour of the Sun Temple at Konarak. Mrs. Das (Mina) confesses that Bobby is not her husband's son and asks if Mr. Kapasi feels she has pain or guilt. Mr. Kapasi's 'other job' interpreting Gujarati in a doctor's office fascinates Mrs. Das. The story ends with a slip of paper with Mr. Kapasi's address blowing away in the wind after monkeys attack Bobby and Mr. Kapasi saves him. Mr. Kapasi had left his grammar school job after his seven-year-old son died of typhoid.

clues.clue: Mr. Kapasi gives tour; saves Bobby from monkeys; address blows away in wind
examples: Mr. Kapasi chases off monkeys that attack Bobby

clues.clue: Mr. Kapasi left grammar school after son died of typhoid

works.name: A Temporary Matter
works.description: The first story in Interpreter of Maladies, about the estranged couple Shoba and Shukumar who share secrets during periodic power outages/blackouts. Shukumar confesses returning a sweater and ripping a woman's photo from a magazine. Shoba reveals she signed a lease on a new apartment. Shukumar retaliates by revealing that their stillborn child was a boy — a fact Shoba had refused to learn. The story ends with them weeping 'for the things they now knew.'

clues.clue: Shoba and Shukumar share secrets during power outages; stillborn baby revealed to be a boy
examples: a couple have a candlelit dinner during a power outage

clues.clue: Ends with couple weeping 'for the things they now knew'

works.name: Sexy
works.description: A story from Interpreter of Maladies in which the married Indian man Dev whispers the title word into Miranda's ear at the Mapparium. Miranda's co-worker Laxmi tells her about Indian culture. The boy Rohin, who is trying to memorize world capitals, defines the title word as 'loving someone you don't know,' which leads Miranda to break off her extramarital relationship. Miranda bemoans she can't wear a cocktail dress as Dev's mistress.

clues.clue: Dev whispers 'sexy' at the Mapparium; Rohin defines it as 'loving someone you don't know'
examples: Rohin defines the title word as 'loving someone you don't know'

works.name: The Namesake
works.description: A novel about Gogol Ganguli, named after the Russian writer because his father Ashoke survived a train crash while clutching a crumpled page from Gogol's 'The Overcoat.' As an architecture student at Yale, Gogol legally changes his name to Nikhil. He later divorces Moushumi. The novel follows the Ganguli family's immigrant experience.

Ashoke suggests the name 'Gogol' for his and Ashima's child

examples: the protagonist of a novel by this author learns of a train crash that leaves his father with only a crumpled page from a Russian short story

works.name: The Lowland
works.description: A novel about two brothers, Subhash and Udayan, who grow up in Calcutta. Udayan joins the Naxalite movement and is shot dead by paramilitary police hiding in the water. Subhash moves to Rhode Island for his PhD and marries Udayan's pregnant widow Gauri, raising the brother's daughter Bela as his own. Gauri later abandons Bela to move to California and write a dissertation on the Naxalites. The brothers once got caught sneaking into the Tollyclub golf course.

clues.clue: Brothers Subhash (Rhode Island PhD) and Udayan (Naxalite, killed by police)
examples: one who moves to Rhode Island for his Ph.D., and the other who joins the Naxalite terrorist movement before being shot dead by policemen

clues.clue: Brothers caught sneaking into the Tollyclub golf course

works.name: Unaccustomed Earth
works.description: Lahiri's second story collection, titled from a Nathaniel Hawthorne phrase in 'The Custom-House.' The title story follows Ruma in Seattle, whose widower father visits and she discovers his relationship with Mrs. Bagchi when he loses a postcard. Her son Akash steals a postcard to plant in a garden he makes with his grandfather. The collection includes a trio of linked stories about Hema and Kaushik: in 'Once in a Lifetime,' Kaushik's family moves because his mother is dying of breast cancer; in 'Going Ashore,' Kaushik becomes a photojournalist killed by a tsunami at a Thai resort. 'Hell-Heaven' features the graduate student Pranab following young Usha. 'Only Goodness' has a woman finding her brother passed out drunk beside her son's bath.

clues.clue: Title from Hawthorne's 'The Custom-House'; Ruma in Seattle; father's relationship with Mrs. Bagchi
examples: a collection by this author titled for a phrase from Nathaniel Hawthorne's 'The Custom-House'

clues.clue: Grandfather and grandson garden; son steals postcard; father chastises about delphiniums

works.name: Other Stories from Interpreter of Maladies
works.description: Additional stories from the Interpreter of Maladies collection. 'Mrs. Sen's' features a woman who crashes into a telephone pole while trying to buy fish, after which she stops taking care of the boy Eliot. 'This Blessed House' follows Sanjeev and Twinkle, who find Christian paraphernalia (including a bust of Christ) in their new attic. 'When Mr. Pirzada Came to Dine' has the title character ritually bringing candy every night until Halloween. 'The Third and Final Continent' features a 103-year-old Mrs. Croft who exclaims 'She's a perfect lady!' and makes the narrator praise the moon landing as 'Splendid.' 'The Treatment of Bibi Haldar' has a doctor concluding that marriage will cure the unstable girl.

clues.clue: 'Mrs. Sen's': crashes into telephone pole buying fish; stops caring for Eliot
examples: After crashing into a telephone pole while trying to buy fish, the title woman stops taking care of Eliot

clues.clue: 'This Blessed House': Twinkle and Sanjeev find Christian paraphernalia in attic

a woman whose husband reminds her she is not Christian after she exhibits religious trinkets

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Lahiri is a Bengali-American author who later moved to Rome and began writing exclusively in Italian. Her novel Whereabouts (Dove Mi Trovo) was originally written in Italian, about an unnamed woman wandering an unnamed European city. She translated it herself into English. Her essay 'Teach Yourself Italian' describes her decision to abandon English, comparing the Italian language to 'cradling a newborn' and English to a 'hairy, smelly teenager.' Ann Goldstein translated her essays In Other Words into English. She praised a Michael F. Moore translation of Manzoni's The Betrothed.

clues.clue: Moved to Rome; began writing exclusively in Italian; Whereabouts originally in Italian
examples: this author now writes only in Italian
After moving to Rome, this author published the 2018 novel Whereabouts in Italian

clues.clue: 'Teach Yourself Italian' essay; compared Italian to 'cradling a newborn,' English to 'hairy, smelly teenager'
examples: that author calls English a 'sense of failure that is the source of almost all my anxiety'

clues.clue: Ann Goldstein translated her essays In Other Words
examples: Goldstein translated a collection of essays by this author titled In Other Words

clues.clue: Bengali-American author
examples: this Bengali-American author who included 'A Temporary Matter' in Interpreter of Maladies

comprehensive_summary:
Jhumpa Lahiri is one of the most frequently clued contemporary American authors in quizbowl, known for fiction exploring the Indian-American immigrant experience. Her debut collection Interpreter of Maladies is extensively clued, especially its title story about the tour guide Mr. Kapasi who saves Bobby Das from monkeys at the Sun Temple at Konarak, whose address blows away in the wind at the end. 'A Temporary Matter' follows the couple Shoba and Shukumar sharing secrets during power outages, culminating in the revelation that their stillborn child was a boy. 'Sexy' features Dev whispering the title word at the Mapparium, with Rohin defining it as 'loving someone you don't know.'

Her novel The Namesake follows Gogol Ganguli, named after the Russian writer because his father Ashoke survived a train crash clutching a page of 'The Overcoat.' Gogol changes his name to Nikhil at Yale and later divorces Moushumi. The Lowland follows the brothers Subhash and Udayan: Udayan joins the Naxalite movement and is killed, while Subhash moves to Rhode Island and raises Udayan's daughter.

Her second collection Unaccustomed Earth (titled from Hawthorne) includes linked stories about Hema and Kaushik, and the title story about Ruma in Seattle. Other notable stories from Interpreter of Maladies include 'Mrs. Sen's' (crashes into pole buying fish), 'This Blessed House' (Twinkle finds Christian objects), and 'The Third and Final Continent' (103-year-old Mrs. Croft exclaims 'Splendid!' about the moon landing).

Lahiri later moved to Rome and began writing exclusively in Italian, publishing Whereabouts (Dove Mi Trovo) in Italian and translating it herself. Her essay 'Teach Yourself Italian' describes abandoning English, comparing Italian to 'cradling a newborn.'
